blocks.content:
The three dots stayed.

Then disappeared.

Then came back again.

Ethan leaned forward slightly, elbows resting on his knees now, phone still in his hand like it might suddenly decide to stop working if he looked away.

He hadn’t felt like this in a while.

Not anticipation.

That wasn’t new.

This was different.

This felt like waiting for something that mattered more than he was used to admitting.

The dots vanished.

A message appeared.

messages.content: You actually sent that like it was normal.

blocks.content:
Ethan let out a small laugh.

That was fair.

messages.content: I met her at the park.

blocks.content:
Ethan stared at that message for a second.

He didn’t respond right away.

Because Nathan wasn’t wrong.

That was the part that kept replaying.

She had stepped into his space.

Into his quiet.

Like it didn’t belong to silence anymore.

messages.content: Then why are you texting me like there is?

blocks.content:
Ethan leaned back slightly, eyes drifting toward the sketchbook again.

The fountain.

Her.

The way everything felt… different now.

The message sent before he could second guess it.

For a moment—

nothing.

Then:

That was it.

Just “oh.”

Ethan frowned slightly.

messages.content: I wasn’t prepared for that level of honesty.
No, no. We’re past that now.

blocks.content:
Ethan didn’t respond right away.

Because that part—

felt heavier than it should have.
